mission.title: Install and Configure Capsule on Kubernetes
mission.description: Capsule is a multi-tenancy and policy-based framework for Kubernetes, designed to simplify the management of multiple namespaces within a single cluster. By using Capsule, you can efficiently manage resources and policies across different tenants, reducing the complexity of cluster sprawl.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Capsule Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add capsule https://projectcapsule.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the Capsule Operator
steps.description:
Install the Capsule Operator using Helm, specifying the desired namespace (e.g., `capsule-system`).
```bash
helm install capsule capsule/capsule --namespace capsule-system --create-namespace --version 0.12.4
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Capsule pods are running correctly.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n capsule-system
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits and RBAC
steps.description:
Set up resource limits and RBAC policies as needed for your environment. This can be done by modifying the values.yaml file or applying custom configurations.
```bash
# Example of applying a resource limit
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: example-limits
  namespace: your-namespace
spec:
  limits:
  - default:
      cpu: 500m
      memory: 512Mi
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: 250m
      memory: 256Mi
    type: Container
EOF
```

steps.title: Set up monitoring (optional)
steps.description:
Integrate monitoring solutions like Prometheus or Grafana to keep track of the Capsule performance and resource usage.
```bash
# Example command to deploy Prometheus
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/prometheus-operator/prometheus-operator/master/bundle.yaml
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Capsule release from your cluster.
```bash
helm uninstall capsule --namespace capsule-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) associated with Capsule.
```bash
kubectl delete crd tenants.capsule.clastix.io
kubectl delete crd namespaces.capsule.clastix.io
```

uninstall.title: Remove the namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the Capsule namespace to clean up any remaining resources.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace capsule-system
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current state of your Capsule installation.
```bash
kubectl get all -n capsule-system -o yaml > capsule-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to ensure you have the latest charts.
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Capsule installation
upgrade.description:
Upgrade the Capsule installation to the latest version.
```bash
helm upgrade capsule capsule/capsule --namespace capsule-system --version 0.12.4
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Capsule pods are running the new version correctly.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n capsule-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in Pending
troubleshooting.description:
If the Capsule pods are stuck in Pending, check for insufficient resources in your cluster.
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n capsule-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Failed to create Tenant
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues creating a Tenant, verify your RBAC permissions.
```bash
kubectl get clusterrolebindings | grep capsule
```

troubleshooting.title: CrashLoopBackOff errors
troubleshooting.description:
If you see CrashLoopBackOff errors, check the pod logs for more details.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n capsule-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Resource quota exceeded
troubleshooting.description:
If you receive resource quota errors, check the resource limits set for your namespaces.
```bash
kubectl get resourcequotas -n <namespace>
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Capsule will have the Capsule Operator running in the `capsule-system` namespace, with all necessary pods in the Running state. You can manage multiple tenants effectively using the Capsule framework.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add capsule https://projectcapsule.github.io/charts
helm repo update

# Example of applying a resource limit
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: example-limits
  namespace: your-namespace
spec:
  limits:
  - default:
      cpu: 500m
      memory: 512Mi
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: 250m
      memory: 256Mi
    type: Container
EOF

# Example command to deploy Prometheus
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/prometheus-operator/prometheus-operator/master/bundle.yaml

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have Kubernetes cluster access and Helm installed.
